refined_fr.refined_fr: Maintain teleoperation link availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold throughout the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR 'achieve the customer-defined teleoperation link availability' is vague about the temporal and comparative nature of the requirement. The refined version clarifies that availability is a sustained property (not a one-time achievement) measured against a specific threshold over a defined period. This removes ambiguity about what 'achieve' means while remaining solution-neutral—it does not prescribe redundancy, protocol choice, or network architecture. The FR is atomic: it expresses a single obligation (maintain availability above threshold) without embedded 'and'/'or' conditions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect teleoperation link uptime logs over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (total_uptime / total_period_duration). Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.999). Verify through operational data, test campaigns, or field trials depending on project phase.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant communication pathways with automatic failover logic to maintain continuous link availability when primary pathway degrades or fails.
DP2: Deploy real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection and proactive rerouting to alternative pathways before availability threshold is breached.

DP3: Establish hierarchical communication architecture with local autonomous fallback mode that preserves critical teleoperation functions during link unavailability, resuming remote control upon link restoration.
DP4: Implement adaptive bandwidth and latency management with dynamic protocol selection based on measured link conditions to sustain availability within acceptable performance bounds.
DP5: Combine redundant pathways (DP1) + link quality monitoring (DP2) + autonomous fallback (DP3) as an integrated availability assurance system with coordinated state management.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Integrated availability assurance system combining (1) redundant communication pathways with automatic failover, (2) real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection, and (3) hierarchical architecture with local autonomous fallback mode, coordinated through unified state management and switchover logic.

potential_sources: State synchronization between remote operator interface and local autonomous fallback mode—mitigated by explicit state handoff protocol and versioning.
Failover decision logic must coordinate across redundancy manager, quality monitor, and fallback controller—mitigated by clear priority rules and event-driven architecture.
Pathway selection (DP1) and protocol adaptation (DP4) may conflict if not decoupled—recommend DP4 operate within each pathway independently rather than as global override.
Monitoring overhead (DP2) must not consume bandwidth needed for teleoperation—mitigated by out-of-band or low-bandwidth heartbeat channels.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 is composite but exhibits low coupling if subsystems (redundancy, monitoring, fallback) are designed with clear interfaces and independent failure modes. Recommend explicit separation of concerns: redundancy manager owns pathway selection; quality monitor owns threshold detection; fallback controller owns autonomous mode logic. Shared state (link status, mode) must be immutable or transactional.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined target threshold (≥0.999) is technically achievable with available communication infrastructure and does not require exotic or cost-prohibitive redundancy.

RISK: Evaluation period duration not specified—short periods (hours) may not capture rare failure modes; long periods (months) require sustained operational discipline. Recommend clarification of evaluation window.

RISK: 'Link availability' definition ambiguous—does it include latency, jitter, or only binary connectivity? Recommend explicit SLA definition (e.g., 'link available if RTT < 500ms and packet loss < 1% for ≥99.9% of measurement intervals').
RISK: Autonomous fallback mode (DP3) may not be acceptable for all teleoperation tasks (e.g., precision manipulation). Recommend scoping which functions remain available during link loss.
RISK: Redundant pathways imply multiple communication channels (e.g., cellular, satellite, wired)—cost, power, and physical integration constraints not yet addressed.
RISK: Predictive degradation detection (DP2) requires historical baseline and ML/statistical models—model accuracy directly impacts false-positive failover rate and operator trust.
ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan permits autonomous fallback operation without continuous remote supervision; if not, DP3 scope must be restricted.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to: 'Maintain teleoperation link availability at or above [customer-defined target, e.g., 0.999] as measured by [explicit metric: binary connectivity | RTT + packet loss | application-level heartbeat] over [evaluation period: e.g., 30 consecutive operational days], with graceful degradation to [specified autonomous fallback scope] during link unavailability, and automatic recovery to full remote control upon link restoration.' This revision clarifies the boundary between DP5 subsystems and enables testability. Additionally, recommend explicit acceptance criteria for failover latency (e.g., 'switchover to fallback mode within 2 seconds of link loss detection') and operator notification protocol.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for managing remote vehicle control, communication link integrity, failover coordination, and autonomous fallback modes during communication degradation or loss.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface for monitoring redundant communication pathways, link quality metrics, and triggering failover between primary and secondary channels.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound stream of vehicle state and link quality telemetry data for real-time degradation detection and predictive analysis.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound control commands to Vehicle; switches between operator-directed and autonomous fallback tasking based on link state.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound safety notifications when communication degradation or failover events occur, triggering Safety Services intervention if needed.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Teleoperation owns the operator-vehicle communication link, failover logic, and autonomous fallback modes. Unified state management and switchover logic naturally reside here. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert notifications couple to Safety Services, but this is a clean, well-defined boundary.

Moderate fit: Safety Services could own failover and fallback logic as safety-critical functions, but it would not naturally own real-time link quality monitoring or redundant pathway management. Significant diffusion: communication pathway logic, network state management, and operator coordination would leak into Teleoperation and Vehicle modules.

Poor fit: Vehicle module should focus on vehicle dynamics, actuation, and local autonomy. Placing unified availability assurance, link monitoring, and operator failover logic here violates separation of concerns. High diffusion: operator coordination, communication pathway management, and state synchronization would diffuse across Teleoperation and NGHE Solution.

Weak fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not a functional module. Placing DP5 here would make it a system-level concern rather than a localized, ownable module. High diffusion: implementation would fragment across Teleoperation, Vehicle, and Safety Services without clear ownership.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of DP5 because it is responsible for maintaining the operator-vehicle communication contract. Redundant pathways, link quality monitoring, failover logic, and autonomous fallback modes are all mechanisms to preserve this contract under degraded conditions. The unified state management and switchover logic coordinate these mechanisms and belong in the module that owns the communication relationship.
containment_risks: Risk: Tight coupling between Teleoperation and Network Link interface. If network abstraction is weak, link-specific failover logic may become entangled with operator control logic. Mitigation: Define Network Link interface as a clean contract (e.g., link status, quality metrics, failover commands) to isolate network details.

Risk: Autonomous fallback mode logic may require deep knowledge of Vehicle capabilities and constraints. If Vehicle module does not expose a clear autonomous tasking interface, fallback logic in Teleoperation may become implementation-specific. Mitigation: Ensure Tasking interface supports both operator-directed and autonomous modes with clear semantics.

Risk: Real-time link quality monitoring and predictive degradation detection may require low-latency processing. If Teleoperation module is not designed for real-time operation, latency may degrade failover responsiveness. Mitigation: Ensure Teleoperation is architected for real-time constraints (e.g., deterministic scheduling, bounded processing).

Risk: SafetyAlert coupling to Safety Services may create circular dependencies if Safety Services needs to query Teleoperation state. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert as a unidirectional notification interface; Safety Services should not pull state from Teleoperation.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR specifies a measurable acceptance criterion (link availability ≥ 0.999 over agreed evaluation period). The Teleoperation IM boundary owns the mechanisms (redundant pathways, link quality monitoring, failover logic, autonomous fallback) required to achieve and demonstrate this threshold. Verification can be conducted through telemetry logging, failover event injection testing, and sustained operational measurement. The Network Link, Telemetry, and Tasking interfaces provide observable signals for validation.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated to the Teleoperation module with well-defined ownership of the operator-vehicle communication contract. The four external interfaces (Network Link, Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) establish unambiguous responsibility boundaries. The containment score of 0.92 for Teleoperation reflects strong fit; the 0.08 diffusion score is acceptable and limited to a clean, unidirectional SafetyAlert notification boundary.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 logically and completely addresses the FR. The three pillars—(1) redundant communication pathways with automatic failover, (2) real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection, and (3) hierarchical architecture with local autonomous fallback—directly map to mechanisms for maintaining availability above the customer-defined threshold. The unified state management and switchover logic coordinate these mechanisms coherently. No logical gaps or contradictions detected between FR intent and DP specification.

diffusion_risks: Network Link abstraction risk: If the Network Link interface does not cleanly abstract redundant pathway details (e.g., primary/secondary channel status, quality metrics, failover commands), link-specific logic may leak into Teleoperation's operator control logic. Mitigation: Enforce a contract-based interface definition (e.g., LinkStatus enum, QualityMetrics struct, FailoverCommand) to isolate network implementation details.

Autonomous fallback mode coupling: Fallback logic in Teleoperation requires knowledge of Vehicle autonomous tasking capabilities and constraints. If the Tasking interface does not clearly distinguish operator-directed vs. autonomous modes with explicit semantics, fallback logic may become tightly coupled to Vehicle implementation. Mitigation: Define Tasking interface with explicit mode tags (e.g., OperatorDirected, AutonomousFallback) and ensure Vehicle exposes autonomous capability boundaries.

Real-time latency risk: Link quality monitoring and predictive degradation detection must operate within bounded latency to ensure failover responsiveness. If Teleoperation is not architected for real-time constraints (deterministic scheduling, priority queues, bounded processing), failover latency may exceed acceptable thresholds and degrade availability. Mitigation: Specify real-time scheduling requirements and validate through latency profiling and stress testing.

SafetyAlert circular dependency risk: If Safety Services queries Teleoperation state in response to SafetyAlert notifications, a circular dependency may emerge, complicating state consistency and testing. Mitigation: Enforce unidirectional SafetyAlert interface; Safety Services must not pull state from Teleoperation. Define notification payload to include sufficient context for Safety Services decision-making.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation is the optimal IM owner for DP5 (containment score 0.92). The module naturally owns the operator-vehicle communication contract and the mechanisms to preserve it under degraded conditions. Unified state management and switchover logic belong in the module responsible for the communication relationship. The four identified diffusion risks are manageable through interface discipline and real-time architecture constraints. No alternative module (Safety Services, Vehicle, NGHE Solution) offers better containment without introducing higher diffusion or violating separation of concerns.

recommended_revisions: Formalize the Network Link interface contract: Define a schema for link status (e.g., Primary, Secondary, Degraded, Lost), quality metrics (latency, packet loss, signal strength), and failover commands. This isolates network abstraction and prevents link-specific logic from diffusing into operator control logic.

Extend the Tasking interface with explicit mode semantics: Add mode tags (OperatorDirected, AutonomousFallback) and capability constraints to enable Teleoperation to select appropriate fallback behavior without tight coupling to Vehicle implementation details.

Specify real-time scheduling and latency requirements for Teleoperation: Define maximum acceptable latency for link quality monitoring, degradation detection, and failover decision-making. Validate through deterministic scheduling analysis and latency profiling under load.

Enforce unidirectional SafetyAlert notification: Document that Safety Services must not query Teleoperation state; all required context must be included in the SafetyAlert payload. Add a note to the interface definition prohibiting pull-based state access.

Add a link availability measurement and logging subsystem within Teleoperation: Ensure continuous telemetry capture of link state transitions, failover events, and quality metrics to support verification against the ≥0.999 acceptance criterion. Define data retention and analysis procedures.

Document autonomous fallback mode decision logic: Specify the conditions under which Teleoperation transitions from operator-directed to autonomous fallback (e.g., link quality threshold, timeout duration) and the criteria for returning to operator control. This clarifies the boundary between Teleoperation and Vehicle responsibilities.

decision.justification: The FR5 and DP5 pass both logical and allocation gates with high confidence. However, the dp_im_gate identifies four material diffusion risks that are manageable but require explicit architectural controls. Given the high safety criticality of the teleoperation domain, decomposing FR5 into sub-FRs that directly address these risk vectors is justified. This approach ensures that interface contracts, real-time constraints, and fallback mode semantics are elevated to explicit functional requirements rather than buried in implementation details. Decomposition will also enable clearer verification strategies for each risk mitigation.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Provide a clean, contract-based abstraction of redundant network pathways and link quality metrics to Teleoperation operator control logic, isolating network-specific implementation details and preventing logic diffusion.

FR5.2: Enable Teleoperation to select and execute appropriate autonomous fallback behavior by exposing Vehicle autonomous tasking capabilities and constraints through explicit, mode-tagged interface semantics (OperatorDirected vs. AutonomousFallback).

FR5.3: Ensure link quality monitoring, degradation detection, and failover decision-making operate within bounded, deterministic latency constraints to maintain failover responsiveness and preserve link availability above the 0.999 threshold.

FR5.4: Provide unidirectional, context-rich SafetyAlert notifications from Teleoperation to Safety Services, eliminating circular state dependencies and enabling Safety Services to make autonomous safety decisions without querying Teleoperation state.

FR5.5: Continuously measure, log, and report link availability metrics (state transitions, failover events, quality time-series) to support verification against the ≥0.999 acceptance criterion and enable post-incident analysis.

revise.why: No revision of FR5, DP5, or IM allocation is required at this level. The gates pass; the recommended revisions from the dp_im_gate are architectural controls that will be addressed through sub-FR decomposition and interface formalization in the next level.

assumptions: The Network Link interface can be formalized with a contract-based schema (LinkStatus enum, QualityMetrics struct, FailoverCommand) without requiring changes to underlying network infrastructure.
The Vehicle module exposes autonomous tasking capabilities and constraints through a well-defined interface that can be extended with explicit mode tags.
Teleoperation can be architected to meet real-time scheduling requirements (deterministic scheduling, priority queues, bounded processing) within the constraints of the target deployment platform.
Safety Services can be designed to operate on SafetyAlert notification payloads without requiring pull-based state queries from Teleoperation.
Link availability measurement and logging can be implemented within Teleoperation without introducing unacceptable performance overhead or storage burden.
open_risks: Network Link abstraction risk: If the Network Link interface contract is not rigorously enforced, link-specific logic may leak into Teleoperation's operator control logic, increasing coupling and reducing modularity. Mitigation: FR5.1 explicitly addresses this; interface formalization and contract testing will be required.

Autonomous fallback mode coupling: If the Tasking interface does not clearly distinguish operator-directed vs. autonomous modes, fallback logic may become tightly coupled to Vehicle implementation. Mitigation: FR5.2 explicitly addresses this; mode semantics and capability constraints will be formalized.

Real-time latency risk: If Teleoperation is not architected for real-time constraints, failover latency may exceed acceptable thresholds and degrade availability. Mitigation: FR5.3 explicitly addresses this; latency requirements and scheduling analysis will be required.

SafetyAlert circular dependency risk: If Safety Services queries Teleoperation state in response to SafetyAlert notifications, a circular dependency may emerge. Mitigation: FR5.4 explicitly addresses this; unidirectional interface enforcement and payload design will be required.

Verification feasibility for link availability: The ≥0.999 threshold requires sustained operational measurement over an agreed evaluation period. If link availability logging is not comprehensive or if evaluation methodology is not clearly defined, verification may be contested. Mitigation: FR5.5 explicitly addresses this; measurement subsystem design and verification procedures will be formalized.
evidence_needed: Formal Network Link interface contract (schema, semantics, error handling) with examples demonstrating isolation of network-specific logic.
Extended Tasking interface definition with explicit mode tags (OperatorDirected, AutonomousFallback) and Vehicle autonomous capability constraints.

Real-time scheduling analysis for Teleoperation, including latency budgets for link quality monitoring, degradation detection, and failover decision-making, with validation through deterministic scheduling tools and latency profiling under load.
SafetyAlert interface definition with unidirectional semantics, context-rich payload schema, and documented prohibition on pull-based state access.
Link availability measurement and logging subsystem design, including telemetry capture strategy, data retention policy, and analysis procedures for verifying the ≥0.999 acceptance criterion.
Autonomous fallback mode decision logic specification, including transition conditions (link quality threshold, timeout duration) and criteria for returning to operator control.

Integration and system-level testing plan demonstrating failover responsiveness, link availability measurement accuracy, and SafetyAlert notification reliability under simulated network degradation scenarios.
